domain: multimodal data curation / vision-language training data quality
goal: Improve the image-text pair filtration method: maximize detection_f1 for identifying corrupted (misaligned or degraded) image-caption pairs, beating the CLIP-similarity baseline at a fixed keep ratio.
dataset: 1,200 image-caption pairs sampled deterministically (seed=42) from COCO val2017 (downloaded and cached on first run). 30% of pairs are synthetically corrupted by a frozen, seeded generator using four corruption types: caption swapped with another image's caption, word-order shuffling, truncation to a 3-word fragment, and replacement with a generic template caption. Ground-truth corruption labels are known to the evaluator only.
baseline: CLIP ViT-B/32 cosine similarity between image and caption embeddings; the 30% lowest-scoring pairs are flagged as corrupted (keep ratio fixed at 70%).
constraints: Do NOT modify the data loading, corruption generator, or evaluation logic - only the scoring function (the SCORING section of experiment.py) may be changed
The keep ratio is fixed at (1 - corruption_rate) and must not be changed
A full run must complete within ~10 minutes on a single modest GPU (or ~20 minutes CPU-only)
Network access only for first-run downloads of public models/data (cached thereafter); small pretrained models from HuggingFace are allowed
Must remain a single self-contained experiment.py using PyTorch/transformers
task_description:
This task is based on 'Trust the Model: Compact VLMs as In-Context Judges for Image-Text Data Quality' (Humain, IEEE MLSP 2025). The paper shows that filtering noisy web-scraped image-caption training data with a compact fine-tuned VLM judge produces higher-quality training corpora than CLIP-similarity-based scoring: their filtered 18% subset beat the full dataset on alignment (CLIP sim 0.298 -> 0.313), caption fluency (perplexity 170.2 -> 137.2), and downstream captioning preference (59.4%). The paper explicitly criticizes CLIP-based scoring functions as limited in interpretability and semantic richness, and flags over-filtering (lost diversity) as an open trade-off.

The baseline here is exactly the CLIP-similarity filter the paper argues against. Your job is to propose and implement better scoring functions for detecting corrupted pairs. Promising directions include, but are not limited to: caption fluency signals (e.g., language-model perplexity of the caption, catching shuffles/truncations that CLIP embeddings are insensitive to); hybrid scores combining alignment and fluency signals; caption-structure heuristics (length, grammaticality, generic-phrase detection); object-word grounding checks (do caption nouns appear in the image per CLIP zero-shot classification); score normalization per caption length; or scoring with a small pretrained VLM as a judge (as the paper does). Combining complementary signals is likely to beat any single signal because the four corruption types have different footprints: swapped captions are fluent but misaligned (CLIP catches these), while shuffled/truncated captions may remain lexically 'aligned' but are disfluent (CLIP misses these; a language model catches them).
